words: The following tape was made with Florence Liney Ingle in her home on Bear
Ridge in Idaho.She grew up on Little Bear Ridge, and knew almost all of the
people in the Troy and Kendrick area in the early days. Her father was a farmer
and she married a farmer, King Ingle, and many of her memories and stories
are on this tape of the days of horse farming, thrashing crews, fruit
growing, early schools and cattle rustlers. The interviewer is Rob Moore.
ROB MOORE: When did you come to the county?
FLORENCE LINEY INGLE: In 1884. I mean we came by train. From Wigley, California. That
area. Any
way, was our county seat at that time and they called it an immigrant
train. They were mostly immigrants on it. It wasnft very fancy, you know. Our
seats weren't really They put new seats in there. They had
stoves, you know, I guess in the immigrant trains they always had the stoves
in they heating in those days. Anyway I was only two years old. One
thing I remembered on that train was seeing these strange people.
They were always interesting to me. I'd go from one seat to another, I
suppose... I'd gaze up in their faces. One lady just across the aisle from
where we were setting, she handed me out an orange. I didn't know it was
an orange. Ir heard of lemons, but I didn't know about oranges. I was
only two years old, And I took it over to my folks. Mother took
it and It's an orange." she said.(laughs) That the first time I ever
seen an orange, (laughs) I don't know whether it was growing in California
at that time or not. That was way back in 1884. I doubt very much that
they were growing, they might have been trying some orchard^ out but the
oranges came later. They grew lemons That's why I thought it was a lemon.
It was yellow I'd never seen an orange before.

speaker: RM
words: Uhy did your father leave the area down there?

speaker: FI
words: Father had grown up in Missouri. And they he was interested

speaker: FI
words: in stock back there. And, this Feather River in California

speaker: FI
words: it was rich farm land. And father, I forget how many acres he had there.

speaker: FI
words: It was a large area the farming was quite heavy and he had to do

speaker: FI
words: it with all horses in those days, And he did raise good crops. But out of

speaker: FI
words: 7 crops he lost 3. The river came down from Orville you know, down through

speaker: FI
words: there. Down past Griddley, we were about 8 miles from Griddley. And that

speaker: FI
words: was all a nice valley through there. Well father's land was just in the

speaker: FI
words: right place for the wat to wherethe leva would break up there 1the miners

speaker: FI
words: up levees and they'd break and let the water down on our farm land.

speaker: FI
words: Se father lost 3 crops out of 7. And that was a little discouraging to

speaker: FI
words: him. Our crops were heavy though, he raised very good grain. And very

speaker: FI
words: heavy I mean,This was advertised...he up in

speaker: FI
words: the stock country, he wanted to get in the stock business.

speaker: FI
words: So Idaho, he

speaker: FI

speaker: FI

words: well established and that was one reason why we went there, Qecause this

speaker: FI

speaker: FI

speaker: FI

speaker: FI
words: My brother...

speaker: RM

speaker: FI

speaker: FI

words: to look them up. But they're in my little drawer in there. Wei...

speaker: RM
words: Did your father take out a homestead when he came here?

speaker: FI
words: Yes, he took a homestead but the farm land that he bought, father had sold

speaker: FI
words: his property down there and he had some money you know. We didn't come here

speaker: FI
words: paupers, you know, without anything. And he bought live stock that he

speaker: FI
words: could get along with on the farm.He had bought a couple of cows, and two

speaker: FI
words: horses at first, was all he could get. And he finally picked up more. So

speaker: FI
words: he began finding the land there. It was good farm land. He set out, and

speaker: FI
words: one of the first things he did and he did that before even we came. He

speaker: FI
words: came up here before the family. The summer before. And he set out an

speaker: FI
words: orchard. He found the land where he was going to settle it. Where he was

speaker: FI
words: going to homestead. And he set out an orchard, a big orchard. He set up

speaker: FI
words: all kinds of trees. He set up pear trees, apple trees, peach trees, and

speaker: FI
words: then prune trees, he had quite, he had about 10 acres of prunes on it.

speaker: FI
words: These Italian prunes. They were just plenty then. people that had come before that

speaker: FI
words: had the prunes before, already. They wbre

speaker: FI
words: putting up dryers and drying them, prunes. There was quite a sale for dried

speaker: FI
words: prunes. In those days there were lots of demand for things like that over

speaker: FI
words: in the mines. They'd come to our local stores and fill up. At our local

speaker: FI
words: stores where the supplies the So you could sell prunes and ;

speaker: FI
words: father thought he could dry prunes. He built a^little dryer on the place.

speaker: FI
words: And we had a lot of fun drying. We came in for supper one evening and I

speaker: FI
words: looked out and our dryer was all a blaze so we lost all-our dried prunes

speaker: FI
words: except for some that they'd brought into the house. Oh, that was kinda

speaker: FI
words: a blow to us. He didn't go to worry the dryer he had put on. He'd been

speaker: FI
words: back to Missouri, he'd gotten money from their estate back there. I think

speaker: FI
words: his father had died I believe and they'd allowed him his part in, hts share

speaker: FI
words: in the estate. He'd put most of that money he got into that prune dryer.

speaker: FI
words: So he felt pretty badly about that.

speaker: RM
words: How did it catch?

speaker: FI
words: We never knew. We thought.it was set afire. My brother had just come up

speaker: FI
words: froH there, he'd always looked at it. Well he'd been taking care of it,

speaker: FI
words: the stove, the fucnace down there. And we looked out the door and it was

speaker: FI
words: all ablze. We had some pretty treacherous neighbors in that time. There

speaker: FI
words: was one man especially was treacherous. His wife told us about him because

speaker: FI
words: he was flean to her, very mean to her. She was a good woman. She had relatives

speaker: FI
words: out here at Troy, but he was mean to her and sort of cruel and course she

speaker: FI
words: had hard feeling toward him. He off with another woman. And so she

speaker: FI
words: came up to our house and she told us all the troubles. She told us all

speaker: FI
words: about what he was like. That's the way we got to find out about him. She

speaker: FI
words: said he belonged to the Black Hand, you've haard of that? It's, well, just

speaker: FI
words: a dirty bunch, you know. It was among some of the Swedes and different

speaker: FI
words: people that moved in here. I didn't know of anyone else Lloyd, but his

speaker: FI
words: wife told us that he belonged to that Black Hand bunch.

speaker: RM
words: What did they do?

speaker: FI
words: Oh they just get it in for people and they just do mean things just on

speaker: FI
words: parpose.Not that they had a grudge, but just because they " not be the

speaker: FI
words: same nationality you know, for that matter. No, they're just naturally

speaker: FI
words: mean. And I think I don't know much about it, but I know it's very bad.

speaker: FI
words: It's a very bad group, one of the very worse.

speaker: RM
words: Was part of that Saede and Norwegian conflict?

speaker: FI
words: It's the Swede, he was a Swede. He wa s Swede and he was an uncultured

speaker: FI
words: Swede. He could speak a broken English, his wife is much more intelligent

speaker: FI
words: than he was or better informed. She was a good woman, she was a very good

speaker: FI
words: woman, but I think he made life quite miserable for her. Or she wouldn't

speaker: FI
words: have told us. And she was so broken down about his running off with this

speaker: FI
words: other woman. Leaving her there. I think he was gone a month or so.

speaker: RM
words: He came back?

speaker: FI
words: He had a pickup, and yes he finally came back.(laughs)The woman came back

speaker: FI
words: to her husband, they lived on a little joining place.(laughs)So there

speaker: FI
words: was this Hammond. Her name was Hammond. They left our neighborhood then

speaker: FI
words: of course, they couldn't live there after an episode like that.

speaker: RM
words: This was the Suede?

speaker: FI
words: No, they weren't. I think the Hammonds were Norwegians on one side. I don't

speaker: FI
words: think they were on both sides. I just don't know them well. I didn't knots

speaker: FI
words: them well. But they had two children, very nice children that attended

speaker: FI
words: school with us. But she ... I don't know. She lived in Troy and Troy wasn't

speaker: FI
words: a wild town then.

speaker: RM
words: Who was this?

speaker: FI

speaker: RM

speaker: FI
words: Well so often these little western towns, a lot of people would move into

speaker: FI
words: them. It just seemed like all of them had some very tough characters.

speaker: RM
words: Were there any...

speaker: FI
words: You don't have in those days, nowadays if the towns are so clean compared

speaker: FI
words: to what they used to be. We don't have that sort of thing.

speaker: RM
words: Were there any characters in particular that you remember.

speaker: FI
words: Well I wouldn't like to mention them.

speaker: RM
words: You wouldn't have to mention them by name. Oust things people did.

speaker: FI
words: Well I just never dwelled on anything like that.I usually hear it all.

speaker: FI
words: I was one never to repeat things much and that was one of the rules of

speaker: FI
words: my, our home life. That they could discuss things with us at home but we

speaker: FI
words: weren't to discuss with neighbors or... you know, it makes trouble in the

speaker: FI
words: neighborhood. You can just, people pack things and just keep the neighborhood

speaker: FI
words: all in an uproar, you know. But no, - believed in talking about your

speaker: FI
words: neighbors" at all, saying thay, you know. My mother and father were except

speaker: FI
words: ional people. My mother had had very good bringing up. She lived in Tennessee.

speaker: FI
words: She her, she was, they were Scottish, Schotch, they called them Scotch-Irish.

speaker: FI
words: But they were Scotch people and they're very strict in bringing up the

speaker: FI
words: children and teaching them the Bible and that sort of thing. They were,

speaker: FI
words: and they believed in education, the families as far as they could could

speaker: FI
words: be educated in those days were educated.And mother's sister married a

speaker: FI
words: doctor that was in the neighborhood there. And...

speaker: RM

speaker: FI
words: Doctor was my mother's uncle. Her sister had married, married

speaker: FI
words: this there in Tennessee, and he lived at Greenville, Tennessee.

speaker: FI
words: And his practice was in the surrounding country. And in those days they

speaker: FI
words: rode horseback in the country. And mother said she thought so much of Dr.

speaker: FI
words: because when he had to come out to the coontry, he always came

speaker: FI
words: to their house across, 'cause they were youngsters, you know, and he'd

speaker: FI
words: eat dinner with'em because he like her mother's cooking.(laughs)And her

speaker: FI
words: mother was a wonderful cook I guess. I heard them say that she was. I've

speaker: FI
words: heard her nention things that her mother had cooked and we always thought

speaker: FI
words: my mother was a wonderful cook. Most people that came they always pleased

speaker: FI
words: with the food she cooked. She said she couldn't cook like her own mother.

speaker: FI
words: She could cook as good as her own mother did. So maybe that's what she

speaker: FI
words: thought, but we all everybody thought mother was a good cook.

speaker: RM

speaker: FI

speaker: RM

speaker: FI

speaker: FI
words: had some, you see...

speaker: FI

speaker: FI

speaker: FI
words: I think that there's a creek that still comes down through there. Anyway

speaker: FI
words: there was a sawmill out there. And these people there, people that worked

speaker: FI
words: at the sawmill were good friends of my father and mother. They, there was

speaker: FI
words: usually in those days, the church usually held them together.

speaker: FI
words: There was a little Baptist Church on Burnt Ridge and we all attended church,

speaker: FI
words: go.to Little Bear Ridge to Burnt Ridge to attend church out there and Sunday

speaker: FI
words: School. And they didn't have preachers to go around everywhere. Finally

speaker: FI
words: youngster, just about from 3 to 4 years old, I don't know if I was any

speaker: FI
words: older than that or not. I know I wasn't any older than that. We drove there

speaker: FI
words: to the Sunday School. Fat her was superintendant of the Sunday School.

speaker: FI
words: Other people from Little Bear Ridge drove out there to Sunday School on

speaker: FI
words: Burnt Ridge, it was just a little log house, log cabin there by the side

speaker: FI
words: of the road. I think the highway goes up to just about where the cabin

speaker: FI
words: used to be. I could find it Some people by the name of Morgans

speaker: FI
words: lived just across the road up on a hill above there. And there's one I

speaker: FI
words: think, there's still in Moscow by the name of Morgan that belongs to that

speaker: FI
words: same family. I'm sure he does. Lives out in the north part of Moscow. I

speaker: FI
words: guess they could tell you more than I could because they'd lived on Burnt

speaker: FI
words: Ridge and raised their families. His family grew up there, he was just

speaker: FI
words: when I knew him.

speaker: RM
words: How did the town of Troy get itself going*? What was it like before there

speaker: RM
words: was a town there?

speaker: FI
words: You see, there was a man named Vollmer. He was the big banker in Lewiston.

speaker: RM
words: Did you know Vollmer?

speaker: FI
words: No, my folks did, yes' they knew him very well. He was, I think he was

speaker: FI
words: Jewish. I don't know. But he came from New York to Lewiston. And he built

speaker: FI
words: a big saltbon there. And a marketing house. And he got most of

speaker: FI
words: his money begin with. And they say he started on a barrel of whiskey. A

speaker: FI
words: barrel of whiskey and he divided that barrel up into several and, that's

speaker: FI
words: what they told us.

speaker: RM
words: That's the story they tell about.

speaker: FI
words: Yes that's the story they tell about him. But he got his start with selling

speaker: FI
words: a barrel of whiskey there in Troy.

speaker: RM
words: Troy or Lewiston?

speaker: FI
words: Lewiston, Lewiston yes. Uollmer, they tried awful hard to get something

speaker: FI
words: named for him in Lewiston. But he was a very grasping man. He didn't come

speaker: FI
words: there to build a place up. He came there to make money himself and he did.

speaker: FI
words: He made money hand over fist. The people, he put up a store, a mercantile

speaker: FI
words: store there. And people would come there and run in bills and finally

speaker: FI
words: they couldn't pay their bills and he kicked 'em mad. He hauled 8000,

speaker: FI
words: hundreds of acres in Potlatch Ridge and around Genessce over here. He,

speaker: FI
words: that's how he took people's farms. Now that wasn't good business, you know.

speaker: FI
words: That was good business for him to get the money for him. But to build up

speaker: FI
words: a countryjthat's just not the way to do. If you're going to build a

speaker: FI
words: country you have to build up the farms. And the farmers, instead of getting

speaker: FI
words: everythmg they had. Then rent farms to people that weren't especially

speaker: FI
words: good farmers. And the land got all rundown all around Lewiston and over

speaker: FI
words: here on Genessee that country was just a weed patch. And because he didn't

speaker: FI
words: get the best of farmers on there. Well, he'd get the farm from them and

speaker: FI
words: that's about all he wanted, was the land. And he owned nearly all of that laid

speaker: FI
words: and so Uollmer didn't have a very good name when he came to Troy. And he

speaker: FI
words: started that .

speaker: RM

speaker: FI
words: They didn't call it the First Bank of Troy then. I don't know what they

speaker: FI
words: called the bank then. I think they called it the Uollmer Bank. I'm sure

speaker: FI
words: they called it the Vollmer Bank.

speaker: RM

speaker: FI
words: It was a little village, it was just, people had built all over the hill

speaker: FI
words: side there And they had a church I think the Lutherans had the main church

speaker: FI
words: there. I don't know. I think there was a Methodist church at one time.

speaker: FI
words: And so, but people got down on him because he was just like started at

speaker: FI
words: Troy, just like he did around Genessee and Lewiston. Tatting, getting people

speaker: FI
words: in debt to him and taking their property. That's no good banker. No good

speaker: FI
words: banker will do that. And I'll tell you how. You don't need to use this

speaker: FI
words: for a not£ But just to contrast the difference between a banker of that

speaker: FI
words: sort and a banker that will build up. Martin-Thomas moved ^to Kendrick here.

speaker: FI
words: He lived on American Ridge. He made real good on American Ridge. He took

speaker: FI
words: over the bank. It was one of the first banks of Kendrick, I foret what they

speaker: FI
words: called it.

speaker: RM
words: Farmers Bank?

speaker: FI
words: it was the one before the Farmers. It was the one down there where

speaker: FI
words: Frank Martin, where the post office is. There's where the first bank of

speaker: FI
words: Troy, of Kendrick. But Martin Thomas began to help the farmers. He found,

speaker: FI
words: gave them advise and thell them what they could do and what they couldn't

speaker: FI
words: do. We had farmers that would just buy anything they could buy on

speaker: FI
words: time. Well Thomas didn't believe in this. He saw that they for what

speaker: FI
words: they got. And that's what built up Kendrick and the people around Kendrick.

speaker: FI
words: He was absolutely, and the bank in Kendrick's been like that ever since.

speaker: FI
words: They tried to see if the farmers aren't doing the right thing. They'd give

speaker: FI
words: them advise they should have and tell them what to do. Or to quit rather

speaker: FI
words: than take their property. No, I don't think Martin Thomas would have taken

speaker: FI
words: a man's property for anything. He just started a new trend. And I think

speaker: FI
words: Frank Brocke's followed the same around Troy. Dust the same. Because he

speaker: FI
words: He started under Thomas, Martin Thomas there at Kendrick. I don't

speaker: FI
words: think he liked Martin Thomas very well because they get a little grouchy

speaker: FI
words: and he wasn't very stylish.

speaker: FI
words: He wore good clothes but he didn't think very

speaker: FI
words: much of clothes, about clothes, about how he looked. He was just an old

speaker: FI
words: farmer theflC.And he did have a mind. He had a good financial mind. Because

speaker: FI
words: my husband was a director of the bank there for 20, over 20 years I think.

speaker: FI
words: And he never did have anything to say about Martin Thomas, only the only

speaker: FI
words: thing I heard Isim sayibout Martin Thomas that he didn't like. He said

speaker: FI
words: he put too much confidence in some people. People that he wouldn't have

speaker: FI
words: confidence in. But as long as King was there, he always, my husband, he

speaker: FI
words: would advise Thomas about people And he would take his word for it. Because

speaker: FI
words: my husband was very good at judgingpeople, exceptional I doubt if they've

speaker: FI
words: ever had a better man in Kendrick, just for being able to judging people

speaker: FI
words: and still get along with them and not make them angry. As my husband was,

speaker: FI
words: because I know when King died there was a writeup in the paper that pleased

speaker: FI
words: me because they said that King Ingle, let's see, the favorite of this entire

speaker: FI
words: a good name you know. And most friends I don't know just how

speaker: FI
words: it was worded. I had it someplace, the article. But anyway, they gave

speaker: FI
words: a very good reputation for building, getting along with the people. And

speaker: FI
words: he was. And I know when after he passed away, several of the young men

speaker: FI
words: came and  and said, "You know, King Ingle was a father to

speaker: FI
words: me." There's a lot of violence in the country at that time. Drinking and

speaker: FI
words: leading the boys astray.

speaker: FI
words: And King was just against that, he had been

speaker: FI
words: well brought up and he'd tell them where they'd go and what had happened,

speaker: FI
words: If they kept on. One young man he was a Negro over here, and

speaker: FI
words: I can tell you this, don't put it in writing. He told King, you know they

speaker: FI
words: were great to get together and drink. And King, they offerd King some and

speaker: FI
words: King said, oh no, he didn't think he wanted any. Well this you man said

speaker: FI
words: "If you don't krink, you can't associate with us." This is what King wanted

speaker: FI
words: so he didn't. He said he have any friends in this neighborhood.

speaker: FI
words: Well he wanted to get King as long as he was here I guess. But he finally

speaker: FI
words: got tired of it and left. He owned this place over here and went to California

speaker: FI
words: and finally died you know. And just kept on his bullheaded ways, I guess.

speaker: FI
words: He was German and some of them don't ever change, I guess. Something in

speaker: FI
words: their head. They thought if your were going to be a man, you had to drink

speaker: FI
words: beer and liquor with them. So they didn't get King to do that.He was just

speaker: FI
words: as he could be. Oh, he might take a sup of whiskey or brandy,

speaker: FI
words: you know. Oust, you know, to drink. He didn't care for those things. He

speaker: FI
words: hadn't been brought up that way. He wasn't afraid he'd get started, you

speaker: FI
words: know, drinkig, because he knew he wouldn't do that. But most of them would.

speaker: FI
words: They'd get started then they never quit. It sort of ruins in a way,

speaker: FI
words: their character. Because they get to associate with that element of people.

speaker: FI
words: And it makes life just a little bit harder for them than if they had gone

speaker: FI
words: their own way. Most of them, you find out you just couldn't find a better

speaker: FI
words: man than King Ingle. People that lived here will tell you that. He was

speaker: FI
words: my husband and^never could see any that coul take his place with me.(laughs)

speaker: FI
words: just how fine he was, you know. How good and how interested, in people

speaker: FI
words: It wasn't that he just wanted to critize people. He didn't want to see

speaker: FI
words: them ruin themselves. By picking up things that they've make habits of

speaker: FI
words: and they probably would never do it again. Well that's the kind of man

speaker: FI
words: my husband was. So we had a happy life together. He was a good worker,

speaker: FI
words: a good planner and a good business emblem. That was exactly the kind of

speaker: FI
words: man and if he wanted to something, he'd do it.

words: Could you tell me more about the early days of Troy?

speaker: FI
words: I didn't live there, but someone that did live there could tell you so

speaker: FI
words: mcuh more.

speaker: FI
words: We were about, oh Little Bear Ridge I could 'cause I lived on Little Bear

speaker: FI
words: Ridge, I knew the people, all the people on Little Bear Ridge. Well

speaker: FI
words: some of our first settlers there were Swedish people. qood Swedish

speaker: FI
words: people though. I mean, there weren't enough of them that they were clannish.

speaker: FI
